subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
title: Key Camel Breeds in India
focus: Characteristics, habitats, and conservation status of single-humped and double-humped camels in India.
rationale: Distinct topic covering various regional camel breeds, especially the unique Kharai and Bactrian camels, which are highly testable for UPSC environment and geography sections.
sectionExcerpt: Details single-humped dromedary camels like Bikaneri, Jaisalmeri, Mewari, Kachchhi, and the swimming Kharai camel of Gujarat (preserved by Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes). Also covers the double-humped Bactrian camel found in Ladakh, known for its cold desert adaptations, and notes that the wild Bactrian camel is Critically Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Kharai camels can swim up to 3 km in seawater, survive by grazing on mangroves, and are preserved by Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes.
Domestic Bactrian camels are found exclusively in the high-altitude cold desert of Ladakh in India.
Bactrian camels have two humps acting as energy (fat) reservoirs, not water.
Wild Bactrian Camel (Camelus ferus) is a distinct species listed as Critically Endangered by IUCN.

subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
title: Wild and Domestic Yak
focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of Wild Yak (Bos mutus) and Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens).
rationale: Clear species profile with specific IUCN/WPA statuses and geographical distribution, making it a standard environment question candidate.
sectionExcerpt: Discusses the first-ever chromosome-level genome assembly of the Indian/Domestic Yak. Details the Wild Yak (Bos mutus), native to the Tibetan plateau, China, and India (regionally extinct in Bhutan), listed as Vulnerable (IUCN) and Schedule I (WPA). Contrasts with the Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens), known as the 'ship of the Himalayas'.
evidenceBullets: Wild Yak (Bos mutus) is native to China and India but is regionally extinct from Bhutan.
Wild Yak conservation status: IUCN Vulnerable (VU), WPA Schedule I, CMS Appendix I.
Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens) has larger lungs and heart than cattle to adapt to high altitudes.

subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
title: Tamil Nadu's Endangered Species Conservation
focus: Conservation status and key traits of four specific species targeted by Tamil Nadu's conservation fund.
rationale: Highly testable cluster of four distinct species with varying IUCN statuses, endemicity, and unique characteristics (e.g., 'Tiger of Kaveri').
sectionExcerpt: Tamil Nadu sanctioned Rs 1 crore for conserving four species: Lion-tailed macaque (Endangered, endemic to Western Ghats), Madras Hedgehog (Least Concern, endemic to India, known as 'thorny rat'), Striped Hyaena (Near Threatened, scavenger declining in Mudumalai), and Hump-headed mahseer fish (Critically Endangered, endemic to India, called 'Tiger of Kaveri').
evidenceBullets: Lion-tailed macaque is Endangered (IUCN), Schedule I (WPA), and endemic to the Western Ghats.
Madras Hedgehog is Least Concern (IUCN), endemic to India, and is a nocturnal species known locally as the 'thorny rat'.
Striped Hyaena is Near Threatened (IUCN) and acts as a natural scavenger regulating diseases.
Hump-headed mahseer fish is Critically Endangered (IUCN) and is known as the 'Tiger of Kaveri'.

subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
title: Van (Sanrakshan Evam Samvardhan) Amendment Rules, 2025
focus: Key provisions and changes introduced in the 2025 amendment to the forest conservation rules.
rationale: Important legislative/regulatory update regarding Compensatory Afforestation and mining in forest areas, a frequent theme in UPSC polity and environment sections.
sectionExcerpt: Highlights the MoEFCC's Van (Sanrakshan Evam Samvardhan) Amendment Rules, 2025. Key changes include allowing Compensatory Afforestation (CA) land to be transferred to the Forest Department or notified as a protected area. It mandates double the extent of CA on degraded forest land for mining critical/strategic minerals and allows state governments to grant initial 'working permission' for linear projects.
evidenceBullets: Compensatory Afforestation (CA) land can now be transferred to the Forest Department or notified as a protected area under the Indian Forest Act, 1927.
Mining of critical/strategic minerals requires CA on degraded forest land at a minimum of double the extent.
State governments are allowed to grant initial 'working permission' for linear projects like roadworks, railway tracks, and transmission lines.
